_note: India-aware misconceptions and their evidence-backed corrections. Each entry MUST be reviewed by a credentialed clinician before publishing. Citations point to peer-reviewed or authoritative clinical sources.

entries.myth: Semen retention strengthens the body, mind, and 'vital force'.
entries.context_in: A common claim across pop wellness, social media, and parts of traditional discourse in India. Sometimes framed as 'dhat'.
entries.fact: Ejaculation has no measurable negative effect on physical strength, intelligence, or longevity. There is no validated benefit to retention. The term 'dhat syndrome' refers to a culture-bound distress about perceived semen loss — distress is real and treatable, but the underlying biological premise is unsupported.
entries.what_helps: If anxiety about semen, masturbation, or sexual function is causing distress, talk to a clinician — these concerns respond well to psychoeducation and short-term therapy.
sources: Sumathipala, A., et al. (2004). Culture-bound syndromes: the story of dhat syndrome. Br J Psychiatry.
Mehta, V., et al. (2009). Dhat syndrome: a useful diagnostic entity in Indian culture.

entries.myth: White vaginal discharge is a sign of weakness or means something is wrong.
entries.context_in: Often taught as 'leucorrhoea' anxiety in informal Indian settings. Triggers significant shame and unnecessary medication-seeking.
entries.fact: Most vaginal discharge is normal and changes through the menstrual cycle. It only needs medical attention if it is itchy, has a strong odour, is grey/green/yellow, or comes with pelvic pain or bleeding.
entries.what_helps: If there are no symptoms beyond regular discharge, no treatment is needed. If something feels off, see a gynaecologist — not a chemist.
sources: ACOG Vaginitis Practice Bulletin.
RCOG Vaginal discharge guidance.

entries.myth: An intact hymen proves virginity; a torn hymen proves sexual activity.
entries.context_in: Drives 'first-night' fears, 'two-finger tests' (banned in India by Supreme Court 2013) and harmful family pressure.
entries.fact: The hymen is a thin, variable membrane. Many people are born without a noticeable hymen, and it can stretch or tear from sport, tampons, or daily activities — or not at all from intercourse. It cannot be used to determine virginity. The two-finger test is unscientific and was banned in India.
entries.what_helps: Believe people. The presence or absence of bleeding on a wedding night says nothing about anyone's history.
sources: WHO (2018). Eliminating virginity testing: an interagency statement.
Lilaramani v. State of Maharashtra (Indian Supreme Court, 2013) — ban on two-finger test.

entries.myth: A 'tight' vagina means a woman is virginal or hasn't had many partners.
entries.context_in: Pop-culture and porn-driven myth that fuels shame, surgical procedures of dubious benefit, and abusive comparisons.
entries.fact: Vaginal muscle tone varies with arousal, age, hormones, childbirth, and pelvic-floor strength — not 'use'. Vaginas are not stretched permanently by sex with partners.
entries.what_helps: If pelvic-floor issues are present (urinary incontinence, prolapse), pelvic-floor physiotherapy is the evidence-based treatment.
sources: Bo, K., et al. (2017). Pelvic floor muscle training. Cochrane review.

entries.myth: Real men are always ready for sex.
entries.context_in: Masculinity scripts globally and in India, often pressuring men into hiding sexual difficulties or not seeking help.
entries.fact: Healthy adult sexual response is variable. Stress, sleep, mood, medications, alcohol, hormones, and relationship dynamics all change desire and arousal. Difficulty with erections, desire, or ejaculation is common — not a defect of masculinity.
entries.what_helps: Talking to a urologist or sex therapist. ED, low desire, and PE all have effective, evidence-based treatments.
sources: Rosen, R. C., et al. (2004). The MMAS study on aging male sexuality.
ESSM Guidelines on Erectile Dysfunction.

entries.myth: Watching porn always means you are 'addicted' or that something is wrong.
entries.context_in: Pop framing of any porn use as pathology causes shame and discourages people from seeking help when use is genuinely problematic.
entries.fact: Most adults who watch porn do so without harm. A subset experience compulsive sexual behaviour — a real ICD-11 diagnosis (6C72) when use is out of control AND causes significant distress or harm. Distress itself, especially driven by religious or moral conflict, is not the same as compulsivity and is treated differently.
entries.what_helps: If porn use is interfering with relationships, work, or wellbeing, talk to a sex therapist. If the distress is from moral conflict rather than loss of control, that is also addressable in therapy.
sources: ICD-11 6C72 — Compulsive Sexual Behaviour Disorder.
Grubbs, J. B., et al. (2020). Pornography Problems Due to Moral Incongruence.

entries.myth: If we really loved each other, we wouldn't need to talk about sex.
entries.context_in: A common belief that drives years of unmet needs and resentment — particularly in cultures where sex is hard to talk about.
entries.fact: Couples with the most satisfying sex lives talk about sex the most. Communication isn't a sign that love has failed; it's the mechanism by which intimacy grows.
entries.what_helps: Start small: name one thing you'd like more of and one you'd like less of. The Gottman 'soft start-up' format is a good template.
sources: Mark, K. P., & Jozkowski, K. N. (2013). The Mediating Role of Sexual and Nonsexual Communication.
Gottman, J. M. The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work.

entries.myth: Asexuality isn't a real orientation — they just haven't met the right person.
entries.context_in: Common dismissal globally and in India.
entries.fact: Asexuality is a recognised sexual orientation across the major clinical bodies. People on the asexual spectrum may experience little, situational, or no sexual attraction; this is not a disorder, a delay, or a phase.
entries.what_helps: If someone tells you they are ace, demi, or gray-ace, believe them and ask what they need from you.
sources: AVEN — Asexual Visibility and Education Network.
Bogaert, A. F. (2015). Understanding Asexuality.

entries.myth: Masturbation causes weakness, infertility, acne, or long-term harm.
entries.context_in: A persistent belief worldwide and in India, often tied to 'dhat' anxiety and moral messaging.
entries.fact: Masturbation is a common, normal behaviour across ages and genders with no proven physical harm. It does not cause infertility, blindness, acne, hair loss, or weakness. For many people it supports sleep, stress relief, and body awareness.
entries.what_helps: If guilt or compulsive patterns are causing distress, a sex therapist can help separate moral conflict from genuine loss of control — both are addressable.
sources: Robbins, C. L., et al. (2011). Prevalence of masturbation and associated factors. Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med.
Planned Parenthood — Masturbation health information.

entries.myth: Penis size is the main thing that determines a partner's satisfaction.
entries.context_in: Heavily reinforced by porn and advertising for 'enhancement' products.
entries.fact: Most studies find that penis size is a minor factor in partner satisfaction compared with communication, emotional connection, foreplay, and technique. The vagina's most nerve-rich areas are in the outer third, and the clitoris — not depth — is central to most orgasms.
entries.what_helps: Focus on communication and broadening your definition of sex beyond penetration. Anxiety about size responds well to psychoeducation.
sources: Veale, D., et al. (2015). Am I normal? A systematic review of penis size. BJU Int.
Štulhofer, A. (2006). How (un)important is penis size? J Sex Med.

entries.myth: Women naturally don't want or enjoy sex as much as men.
entries.context_in: A gender script that leaves many women's desire unexamined and many men confused by 'mismatched' libido.
entries.fact: Desire varies enormously within every gender. Many women experience 'responsive' desire — arousal that builds in response to a good context rather than appearing spontaneously. That is a normal pattern, not a lack of interest. Context, stress, and relationship quality strongly shape desire for everyone.
entries.what_helps: Learning the responsive-desire model (Emily Nagoski's work) reframes a lot of needless worry. Persistent distress about desire is treatable.
sources: Basson, R. (2000). The female sexual response: a different model. J Sex Marital Ther.
Nagoski, E. (2015). Come As You Are.

entries.myth: You'd obviously know if you or a partner had an STI.
entries.context_in: A dangerous assumption that drives skipped testing and condom-free sex.
entries.fact: Many STIs — including chlamydia, gonorrhoea, HPV, and early HIV — frequently cause no symptoms at all, especially early on. The only way to know your status is to test. Untreated infections can affect fertility and health.
entries.what_helps: Get tested between partners and ask new partners about their last test. Condoms substantially reduce transmission risk.
sources: WHO (2023). Sexually transmitted infections fact sheet.
CDC — STI screening recommendations.

entries.myth: Menopause (or ageing) means the end of sex and desire.
entries.context_in: A widespread assumption that quietly writes off older adults' sexuality.
entries.fact: Many people remain sexually active and satisfied well into later life. Menopause can change lubrication and sensation due to lower oestrogen, but these are treatable, and desire is shaped by far more than hormones. Sex often becomes less performance-focused and more connection-focused with age.
entries.what_helps: Vaginal moisturisers, lubricants, and (where appropriate) local oestrogen help with dryness. A clinician can review options including for libido.
sources: Lindau, S. T., et al. (2007). A study of sexuality and health among older adults. NEJM.
NAMS (North American Menopause Society) position statements.

entries.myth: Good sex means both partners orgasm every time, ideally together.
entries.context_in: A performance standard set largely by film and porn.
entries.fact: Orgasm is not the only measure of good sex, and simultaneous orgasm is uncommon. Many people — especially women — don't orgasm from penetration alone, and that is normal. Pleasure, closeness, and play matter as much as climax.
entries.what_helps: Take orgasm off the scoreboard. Curiosity and communication about what feels good reduce performance pressure for everyone.
sources: Frederick, D. A., et al. (2018). Differences in orgasm frequency. Arch Sex Behav.
Herbenick, D., et al. (2010). National Survey of Sexual Health and Behavior.

entries.myth: Needing lubricant means you're not aroused enough or something's wrong.
entries.context_in: Leads people to avoid lube and push through discomfort.
entries.fact: Natural lubrication varies with hydration, hormones, cycle phase, medications, stress, and age — it isn't a reliable 'arousal meter'. Using lubricant is common, safe, and often makes sex more comfortable and pleasurable. It's a tool, not a verdict.
entries.what_helps: Use a body-safe lubricant (water- or silicone-based). If dryness is persistent and bothersome, a clinician can check for causes like medication side effects.
sources: Herbenick, D., et al. (2011). Association of lubricant use with sexual pleasure. J Sex Med.
ACOG — Experiencing vaginal dryness.
